3 Opening and alleging, that Christ must needs have suffered, and risen again from: the dead; and that this Jesus, whom I preach unto you, is Christ.

Opening and alleging that Christ must needs have suffered, etc.—His preaching, it seems, was chiefly expository, and designed to establish from the Old Testament Scriptures (1) that the predicted Messiah was to be a suffering and dying, and therefore a rising, Messiah; (2) that this Messiah was none other than Jesus of Nazareth. (JFB Bible Commentary)

4 And some of them believed; and consorted with Paul ‘and Silas; and of the devout Greeks a great multitude, and of ‘the chief women ‘not a few.

And of the devout Greeks a great multitude (twn te sebomenwn ellhnwn plhyov polu). These "God-fearers" among the Gentiles were less under the control of the jealous rabbis and so responded more readily to Paul’s appeal. In #1Th 1:9 Paul expressly says that they had "turned to God from idols," proof that this church was mainly Gentile (cf. also #1Th 2:14). (Robertson's NT Word Pictures)

11 These were more noble than those in Thessalonica, in. that. they received the word with all readiness of mind, and searched the seriptures daily, whether those things were 80.

"Search" comes from a root word that means to bend, turn, move around. It has a sense of "turning the land with a plow or turning back and forth as oxen plowing" ("The Origins of English Words"). The Oxford English Dictionary defines search as to explore, examine thoroughly, "to investigate, make oneself thoroughly acquainted with; examine rigorously; to examine, penetrate the secrets of." Search also means to look for or "seek diligently." One of the bedrock commandments in the scriptures…
